entries.description: A character might be offered special training in lieu of a financial reward. This kind of training presumes the existence of a skilled trainer and guarantees a special benefit during downtime.
entries: The character gains inspiration daily at dawn for ld4 + 6 days.
The character gains proficiency in a skill.
The character gains a feat.

entries.name: Epic Boons
entries.description: An epic boon is a special power available only to 20th level characters. Characters at that level gain such boons only if you want them to and only when you feel it's appropriate. Epic boons are best awarded after the characters complete a major quest, or accomplish something else particularly notable. A character might gain an epic boon after destroying an evil artifact, defeating an ancient dragon, or halting an incursion from the Outer Planes. Epic boons can also be used as a form of advancement, a way to provide greater power to characters who have no more levels to gain. With this approach, consider awarding one epic boon to each character for every 30,000 XP he or she earns above 355,000 XP. You determine which epic boon a character gains. Ideally, the boon you pick is something the character would put to use in future adventures. You can allow a player to select a boon for his or her character, subject to your approval. Whatever boon a character ends up with, consider its place in your story and world. Many of the boons are extraordinary and represent the gradual transformation of a character into something resembling a demigod. The acquisition of a boon might visibly transform a character. For example, the eyes of a character with the Boon of True Sight might glow when he or she feels strong emotion, and a character who has the Boon of High Magic might have faint motes of light glimmering around his or her head. Also, decide how the boon first appears. Does the boon appear spontaneously and mysteriously? Or does a being of cosmic power manifest to bestow it? The bestowal of a boon can itself be an exciting scene in an adventure. The text of a boon addresses its user. Unless a boon says otherwise, a character can't gain it more than once.

entries.name: Boon of Combat Prowess
entries.description: When you miss with a melee weapon attack, you can choose to hit instead. Once you use this boon, you can't use it again until you finish a short rest.

entries.name: Boon of Dimensional Travel
entries.description: As an action, you can cast the {@item Misty Step|PHB} spell without using a spell slot or any components. Once you do so, you can't use this boon again until you finish a short rest.

entries.name: BOON OF FATE
entries.description: When another creature that you can see within 60 feet of you makes an ability check, an attack roll, or a saving throw, you can roll a d10 and apply the result as a bonus or penalty to the roll. Once you use this boon, you can't use it again until you finish a short rest.
entries.effect: Roll a d10 and apply the result as a bonus or penalty to the target's roll.

entries.name: Boon of Fortitude
entries.description: Your hit point maximum increases by 40.

entries.name: Boon of High Magic
entries.description: You gain one 9th-level spell slot, provided that you already have one.

entries.name: Boon of Immortality
entries.description: You stop aging. You are immune to any effect that would age you, and you can't die from old age.

entries.name: Boon of Invincibility
entries.description: When you take damage from any source, you can reduce that damage to 0.
limitations: Once you use this boon, you can't use it again until you finish a short rest.

entries.name: BOON OF IRRESISTIBLE OFFENSE
entries.description: You can bypass the damage resistances of any creature.

entries.name: Effect
entries: You can bypass the damage resistances of any creature.

entries.name: Boon of Luck
entries.description: You can add a d10 roll to any ability check, attack roll, or saving throw you make. Once you use this boon, you can't use it again until you finish a short rest.

entries.name: BOON OF MAGIC RESISTANCE
entries.description: You have advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

entries.name: Boon of Peerless Aim
entries.description: You can give yourself a {@bonus +20} to a ranged attack roll you make.
usage: Once you use this boon, you can't use it again until you finish a short rest.

entries.name: Boon of Perfect Health
entries.description: You are immune to all diseases and poisons, and you have advantage on Constitution saving throws.

entries.name: Boon of Planar Travel
entries.description: When you gain this boon, choose a plane of existence other than the Material Plane. You can now use an action to cast {@item Plane Shift|player-handbook.spell-plane-shift} (no spell slot or components required), targeting yourself only, and travel to the chosen plane, or from that plane back to the Material Plane. Once you use this boon, you can't use it again until you finish a short rest.

entries.name: Boon of Quick Casting
entries.description: Choose one of your spells of 1st through 3rd level that has a casting time of 1 action. That spell's casting time is now 1 {@action} for you.

entries.name: BOON OF RECOVERY
entries.description: You can use a bonus action to regain a number of hit points equal to half your hit point maximum. Once you use this boon, you can't use it again until you finish a long rest.

entries.name: Boon of Resilience
entries.description: You have resistance to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from nonmagical weapons.

entries.name: BOON OF SKILL PROFICIENCY
entries.description: You gain proficiency in all skills.

entries.name: Boon of Speed
entries.description: Your walking speed increases by 30 feet. In addition, you can use a bonus action to take the Dash or Disengage action. Once you do so, you can't do so again until you finish a short rest.

entries.name: Boon of Spell Mastery
entries.description: Choose one 1st-level sorcerer, warlock, or wizard spell that you can cast. You can now cast that spell at its lowest level without expending a spell slot.

entries.name: Boon of Spell Recall
entries.description: You can cast any spell you know or have prepared without expending a spell slot. Once you do so, you can use this boon again until you finish a long rest.
entries.usage: Cast any spell you know or have prepared without expending a spell slot. Once used, this boon can be used again until you finish a long rest.

entries.name: Immunity to Fire Damage
entries: You have immunity to fire damage.

entries.name: Spellcasting Ability
entries: You can also cast {@item Burning Hands|PHB} (save DC {@dc 15}) at will, without using a spell slot or any components.

entries.text: While completely in an area of dim light or darkness, you can become invisible as an action. You remain invisible until you take an action or a reaction.

entries.name: Description
entries: You have immunity to {@damage lightning} and {@damage thunder} damage.
You can also cast {@spell thunderwave|PHB} (save DC {@dc 15}) at will, without using a spell slot or any components.

entries.name: Boon of the Unfettered
entries.description: You have advantage on ability checks made to resist being grappled. In addition, you can use an action to automatically escape a grapple or free yourself of restraints of any kind.

entries.name: Boon of Truesight
entries.description: You have truesight out to a range of {@range 60 feet}.

entries.name: Boon of Undetectability
entries.description: You gain a +10 bonus to Dexterity (Stealth) checks, and you can't be detected or targeted by divination magic, including scrying sensors.

entries: If you have the opportunity to plan out a chase, take the time to draw a rough map that shows the route. Insert obstacles at specific points, especially ones that require the characters to make ability checks or saving throws to avoid slowing or stopping, or use a random table of complications similar to the ones in this section. Otherwise, improvise as you play.

entries.name: Complications
entries: Complications can be barriers to progress or opportunities for mayhem. Characters being chased through a forest by bugbears might spot a wasp nest and slow down long enough to attack the nest or throw rocks at it, thus creating an obstacle for their pursuers.

entries.name: Map Complexity
entries: A map of a chase can be linear or have many branches, depending on the nature of the chase. For example, a mine cart chase might have few (if any) branches, while a sewer chase might have several.

entries.name: ROLE REVERSAL
entries.description: During a chase, pursuers may become the quarry. For example, characters chasing a thief through a marketplace might draw unwanted attention from other members of the thieves' guild. As they pursue the fleeing thief, they must also evade the thieves pursuing them. Roll initiative for the new arrivals, and run both chases simultaneously. In another scenario, the fleeing thief might run into the waiting arms of his accomplices. The outnumbered characters might decide to flee with the thieves in pursuit.

entries.name: Ballista
entries.description: A massive crossbow that fires heavy bolts. Before it can be fired, it must be loaded and aimed. It takes one action to load the weapon, one action to aim it, and one action to fire it.

entries.name: Cannon
entries.description: A cannon uses gunpowder to propel heavy balls of cast iron through the air at destructive speeds. It is usually supported in a wooden frame with wheels. Before it can be fired, the cannon must be loaded and aimed. It takes one action to load the weapon, one action to aim it, and one action to fire it.

entries.name: Cauldron, Suspended
entries.description: A cauldron is an iron pot suspended so that it can be tipped easily, spilling its contents. Once emptied, a cauld, must be refilled-and its contents must usually be reheated before it can be used again. It takes three actions to fill a cauldron and one action to tip it. Cauldrons can be filled with other liquids, such as {@item Acid|PHB} or {@item Green Slime|PHB}, with different effects.

special_abilities.name: Boiling Oil
special_abilities.description: The cauldron pours boiling oil onto a 10-foot-square area directly below it. Any creature in the area must make a {@dc 15} Dexterity saving throw, taking {@damage 10 (3d6)} fire damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

entries.name: Mangonel
entries.description: A type of catapult that hurls heavy projectiles in a high arc. This payload can hit targets behind cover. Before firing, it must be loaded and aimed. It takes two actions to load the weapon, two actions to aim it, and one action to fire it.

entries.name: RAM
entries: A ram is a Large object with Armor Class 15, 100 hit points, and damage immunities to poison and psychic damage. It consists of a movable gallery with a heavy iron-shod log suspended by chains, used to break through doors and barricades.
Requires four Medium creatures to operate. Operators have total cover against attacks from above due to the gallery roof.
Melee Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, reach 5 ft., one object. Hit: 16 (3d10) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Massive Damage
entries: When a creature takes damage from a single source equal to or greater than half its hit point maximum, it must succeed on a DC 15 Constitution saving throw or suffer a random effect determined by a roll on the {@table System Shock table}.

entries: The creature drops to 0 hit points.
2-3 The creature drops to 0 hit points but is stable.

8-10 The creature can't take reactions until the end of its next turn.

trait.name: Morale
entries: A creature might flee under any of the following circumstances: The creature is surprised. The creature is reduced to half its hit points or fewer for the first time in the battle. The creature has no way to harm the opposing side on its turn.

A group of creatures might flee under any of the following circumstances: All the creatures in the group are surprised. The group's leader is reduced to 0 hit points, incapacitated, taken prisoner, or removed from battle. The group is reduced to half its original size with no losses on the opposing side.

To determine whether a creature or group of creatures flees, make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw for the creature or the group's leader. If the opposition is overwhelming, the saving throw is made with disadvantage, or you can decide that the save fails automatically. If a group's leader can't make the saving throw for whatever reason, have the creature in the group with the next highest Charisma score make the saving throw instead.

On a failed save, the affected creature or group flees by the most expeditious route. If escape is impossible, the creature or group surrenders. If a creature or group that surrenders is attacked by its conquerors, the battle might resume, and it's unlikely that further attempts to flee or surrender will be made.

action.name: Flee
entries: Flee by the most expeditious route. If escape is impossible, surrender.

action.name: Surrender

action.name: Switch Weapons

Don't forget to change the damage and the attack's reach where appropriate.

Also be aware of the consequences of switching from a one-handed weapon to a two-handed weapon, or vice versa. For example, a hobgoblin wielding a halberd (a two-handed weapon) loses the benefit of its shield, so its AC decreases by 2.

action.name: Defensive Challenge Rating
entries: Read down the Hit Points column of the Monster Statistics by Challenge Rating table until you find your monster's hit points. Then look across and note the challenge rating suggested for a monster with those hit points.

Compare your monster's AC to the AC suggested for that challenge rating. If your monster's AC is at least two points higher or lower, adjust the challenge rating up or down by 1 for every 2 points of difference.

action.name: Offensive Challenge Rating
entries: Read down the Damage Round column of the Monster Statistics by Challenge Rating table until you find your monster's damage output per round. Then look across and note the challenge rating suggested for a monster that deals that much damage.
Compare your monster's attack bonus (or save DC if relying more on saving throws) to the suggested value. Adjust the challenge rating up or down by 1 for every 2 points of difference.
Use the most common attack bonus or save DC if the monster uses different values.

action.name: Average Challenge Rating
entries: The monster's final challenge rating is the average of its defensive and offensive challenge ratings. Round the average up or down to the nearest challenge rating.
Example: Defensive CR 2, Offensive CR 3 → Final CR 3

action.name: Experience Points
entries: Use the Experience Points by Challenge Rating table to determine XP value. A CR 0 monster is worth 0 XP (if non-threatening) or 10 XP.

spellcasting.name: Playtesting

trait.name: Alignment Determination
entries: If your monster has no concept of morals, it is unaligned. Otherwise, it has an alignment appropriate to its nature and moral outlook, as discussed in the Player's Handbook.

trait.name: Ability Scores and Modifiers
entries: Monsters, like player characters, have the six ability scores. A monster can't have a score lower than 1 or higher than 30 in any ability.
A monster's score in any ability determines its ability modifier, as shown in the Ability Scores and Modifiers table in the Player's Handbook.

If you can't decide what a monster's ability scores should be, look for comparable monsters in the Monster Manual and mimic their ability scores. For example, if your monster is roughly as smart as a human commoner, give it an Intelligence of 10 (+0 modifier). If it's as strong as an ogre, give it a Strength of 19 (+4 modifier).

action.name: Armor Class Calculation
entries: A monster's Armor Class has a direct bearing on its challenge rating, and vice versa.

Use the Monster Statistics by Challenge Rating table to choose an appropriate AC based on the monster's expected challenge rating. The table provides the baseline AC for a specific challenge rating. Adjust AC as needed (e.g., raise AC for well-armored monsters).
Don't worry if the monster's AC doesn't match the expected challenge rating; other factors can affect challenge rating.
Determine AC based on armor type, natural armor, or other boosters (e.g., mage armor spell).
For manufactured armor, use Player's Handbook armor types. Add shield bonus if applicable.
Natural armor: AC = 10 + Dexterity modifier + natural armor bonus (e.g., gorgon has +9 natural armor bonus).

trait.name: Attack Bonus Determination
entries: A monster's attack bonuses have a direct bearing on its challenge rating, and vice versa. You can determine a monster's attack bonuses in one of two ways.

Use the Table: Start with the monster's expected challenge rating and use the Monster Statistics by Challenge Rating table to determine an appropriate attack bonus for all the monster's attacks, regardless of its ability scores.

The table provides the baseline attack bonus for each challenge rating. Feel free to adjust the attack bonus as you see fit to match whatever concept you have in mind. For example, the baseline attack bonus for a challenge rating 1 monster is +3, but if your monster needs more accuracy, raise its bonus accordingly.
Calculate Attack Bonuses: Alternatively, calculate a monster's attack bonuses the same way players calculate the attack bonuses of a character.

A monster's attack bonus is equal to its proficiency bonus + its Strength or Dexterity modifier. A monster usually applies its Strength modifier to melee attacks and its Dexterity modifier to ranged attacks, although smaller monsters sometimes use Dexterity for both.
Again, don't worry if the attack bonuses aren't matching up with the expected challenge rating for the monster. You can always adjust a monster's attack bonuses later.

trait.name: Damage Output Calculation
entries: A monster's damage output (amount dealt per round) directly affects its challenge rating. Use the Monster Statistics by Challenge Rating table to determine damage range. The damage can be apportioned any way (single attack, split among attacks).
Choose damage type based on delivery method: slashing for claws, poison for venomous claws, etc. For varying damage per round, convert range to die expressions (e.g., 3d8+4 for CR 2).

trait.name: Weapon-Based Damage
entries: Use weapon die expressions for manufactured weapons (e.g., greataxe = 1d12 + Strength modifier). Oversized weapons deal extra dice: Double for Large, triple for Huge, quadruple for Gargantuan.
Creatures have disadvantage on attack rolls with weapons too large. Weapons sized for attackers two sizes larger are unusable.

trait.name: Overall Damage Output
entries: Average damage per attack and sum for all attacks. Use most effective attacks (e.g., fire giant's greatsword over rock attack).
For variable damage, calculate first three rounds' average (e.g., young white dragon: (90 + 37 + 37) / 3 = 54).
Include special off-turn effects (auras, reactions, legendary actions, lair actions).

action.name: Adjust Damage
entries: Modify damage later if it doesn't match expected CR. Other factors (e.g., defenses, speed) affect CR.

action.name: Ride
entries: Make a DC 13 Wisdom saving throw. On a success, the target gains advantage on their next ability check, attack roll, or saving throw. On a failure, the target is restrained and must make a DC 13 Wisdom saving throw each round to escape. If they fail, they are incapacitated until the end of their next turn.

trait.name: Save DC Calculation
entries: Calculate DCs using: 8 + proficiency bonus + relevant ability modifier. Choose the ability that best applies to the effect.
Example: Poison uses Constitution; spell-like effects may use Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma.

trait.name: DC Determination Methods
entries: Use Monster Statistics by Challenge Rating table to determine save DCs based on expected CR.
Adjust DCs later if they don't align with the monster's challenge rating (other factors may influence CR).

entries.name: Adding Class Levels to Monsters
entries.description: The rules in chapter 3 of the Player's Handbook allow you to grant class levels to monsters. This process modifies the monster's abilities and challenge rating.

entries.name: Example
items: A werewolf becomes a 'Werewolf, 4th-level barbarian' after gaining four barbarian levels

entries.name: Procedure
entries: Start with the monster's stat block and add class features for each level granted
The monster does not gain starting equipment of the added class
Each class level grants one Hit Die (type based on size, ignoring class progression)
Proficiency bonus is determined by challenge rating, not class levels

entries.name: Adjustments
entries: Ability scores can be tweaked after adding class levels
Recalculate challenge rating as if designing the monster from scratch
Challenge rating may increase significantly depending on the class and number of levels added

entries.name: Creating a Spell
entries: If a spell is so good that a caster would want to use it all the time, it might be too powerful for its level.
A long duration or large area can make up for a lesser effect, depending on the spell.

Avoid spells that have very limited use, such as one that works only against good dragons. Though such a spell could exist in the world, few characters will bother to learn or prepare it unless they know in advance that doing so will be worthwhile.

Make sure the spell fits with the identity of the class. Wizards and sorcerers don't typically have access to healing spells, for example, and adding a healing spell to the wizard class list would step on the cleric's turf.

entries.name: Spell Damage Table
entries.description: For any spell that deals damage, use the Spell Damage table to determine approximately how much damage is appropriate given the spell's level. The table assumes the spell deals half damage on a successful saving throw or a missed attack. If your spell doesn't deal damage on a successful save, you can increase the damage by 25 percent. You can use different damage dice than the ones in the table, provided that the average result is about the same. For example, you could change a cantrip's damage from 1d6 (average 5.5) to 2d4 (average 5), reducing the maximum damage and making an average result more likely.

entries.text: Use the Spell Damage table to determine how many hit points a healing spell restores. Cantrips should not offer healing.

entries.name: Modifying an Item
entries.description: Items can be modified by altering their properties, substituting damage types, or combining effects from other items. Examples include changing a holy avenger to a flail, converting fire damage to lightning damage, or merging the effects of multiple items into a single object. Modifications may increase rarity but do not break game balance. Chapter 7, 'Treasure,' provides tools for adding minor properties, quirks, or sentience to items.
modifications: Substituting weapon types (e.g., flail for sword)
Changing damage types (e.g., fire to lightning)
Replacing capabilities (e.g., potion of climbing → potion of stealth)
Fusing item effects (e.g., helm of comprehending languages + helm of telepathy)
Adding minor properties, quirks, or sentience

entries.name: Magic Item Purpose
entries.description: A magic item should either let a character do something they couldn't do before, or improve their ability to do something they already can. {@example} The ring of jumping augments existing movement abilities, while the ring of the ram grants new force damage capability.

entries.name: Item Design Simplicity
entries.description: Simpler item designs are easier to manage in play. Items with charges are acceptable, especially for multi-ability items. {@rule} Prioritize clear usage mechanics like always-active status or fixed daily uses.

Animism is the belief that spirits inhabit every part of the natural world. In an animistic worldview, everything has a spirit, from the grandest mountain to the lowliest rock, from the great ocean to a babbling brook, from the sun and moon to a fighter's ancestral sword. All these objects, and the spirits that inhabit them, are sentient, though some are more aware, alert, and intelligent than others. The most powerful spirits might even be considered deities.

Animists don't typically pay allegiance to one spirit over the others. Instead, they offer prayers and sacrifices to different spirits at different times, as appropriate to the situation. A pious character might make daily prayers and offerings to ancestor spirits and the spirits of the house, regular petitions to important spirits such as the Seven Fortunes of Good Luck, occasional sacrifices of incense to location spirits such as the spirit of a forest, and sporadic prayers to a host of other spirits as well.

An animistic religion is very tolerant. Most spirits don't care to whom a character also offers sacrifices, as long as they receive the sacrifices and respect they are due. As new religions spread through animist lands, those religions typically win adherents but not converts. People incorporate new spirits and deities into their prayers without displacing the old ones. Contemplatives and scholars adopt complex philosophical systems and practices without changing their belief in and respect for the spirits they already venerate.
Animism functions as a large, tight pantheon. Animist clerics serve the pantheon as a whole, and so can choose any domain, representing a favorite spirit for that cleric.

entries.name: Fire-Breath
entries.description: A magical trap activated by a hidden pressure plate. The trap releases a 30-foot cone of fire from a statue when more than 20 pounds of weight is applied. The statue can depict any creature, including dragons or wizards.
entries.activation: Weight (≥20 pounds) on a hidden pressure plate. The plate is difficult to spot (DC 15) and leaves faint scorch marks on the floor and walls.

items: A 30-foot cone of fire erupts from the statue.
Each creature in the cone must make a DC 13 Dexterity saving throw.

prevention: Wedging an iron spike or object under the pressure plate.
Dispel magic (DC 13) cast on the statue destroys the trap.
detection: Detect magic reveals an aura of evocation magic around the statue.

items: DC 10 Wisdom (Perception) to spot the pit
Stepping on the cloth causes falling damage based on pit depth (usually 10 feet)

entries.name: Hidden Pit
entries: Cover constructed from material identical to surrounding floor.

items: DC 15 Wisdom (Perception) to detect absence of foot traffic
DC 15 Intelligence (Investigation) to confirm trapped floor is a pit cover

entries: Trigger causes trapdoor to swing open, dropping creature into pit (10 or 20 feet deep).
Notes: Iron spike or arcane lock can secure pit cover.

entries.name: Locking Pit
entries: Hidden pit trap with spring-loaded cover that snaps shut after a creature falls in.

items: DC 20 Strength check to pry open cover
Smashable cover (use chapter 8 guidelines for statistics)
DC 15 Dexterity (Thieves' Tools) to disable spring mechanism (if reachable and visible)

entries: Notes: Some variants have a mechanism behind a secret door.

entries.name: Spiked Pit
entries: Simple, hidden, or locking pit with sharpened wooden/iron spikes at the bottom.

items: 11 (2d10) piercing damage from spikes + falling damage
22 (4d10) poison damage on failed save (or half on success)

entries.name: Rolling Stone Sphere Trap
entries.description: A mechanical trap activated by 20+ pounds of pressure on a pressure plate, releasing a 10-foot-diameter rolling sphere of solid stone.
activation: Pressure plate triggers the trapdoor when 20+ pounds of weight is applied.
Activation requires all creatures present to roll initiative. The sphere rolls with a +8 bonus.

items: DC 15 Wisdom (Perception) check to spot trapdoor and pressure plate
DC 15 Intelligence (Investigation) check to detect variations in mortar/stone (floor) or stonework (ceiling)

effects: Sphere moves 60 feet in a straight line, treating its space as difficult terrain.
On entering a creature's space or vice versa, creature must succeed on DC 15 Dexterity save or take {@damage 10d10} bludgeoning damage and be {@condition prone}.
Sphere stops at walls; cannot navigate corners but may follow curved passages.
Creatures within 5 feet can use an action to slow it with DC 20 Strength check. Success reduces speed by 15 feet. If speed reaches 0, the sphere stops.
prevention: Wedging an iron spike or similar object under the pressure plate prevents activation.

entries.name: Description
entries: Scrolls are consumable magic items typically stored as rolls of paper, sometimes attached to wooden rods. They are commonly kept in tubes made of ivory, jade, leather, metal, or wood.
A scroll contains arcane script that must be read using an action to activate its magic. Once used, the scroll's magic is expended, causing its words to fade or the scroll to crumble into dust.

entries.name: Usage
entries: Any creature that understands a written language can read a scroll's arcane script and attempt to activate its magic.
Activating a scroll requires an action and consumes the item, rendering it unusable afterward.
